author: @wrekced
text: That is a great idea! Thanks! The only thing I see as a drawback is that rubber decays fairly fast. Especially in heat. I wonder how much energy you could get from a set of springs from large tape measures. I think ten springs in a frame like the one you built for the rubber bands would do the trick. Fix the outer end of the springs to the frame. Attach them all to a bar through the center. Put a handle at one end of the bar and a motor/generator at the other end. I imagine it could run for quite a  while. Especially if the motor you use has a reduction gearbox attached to it like the ones that turn the platter in a microwave oven.  A brake and/or some way to restrain the handle would probably be a good idea. That way you could charge it and store the charge till you need it.

author: @MathewTitus
text:
Just throwing it out there for folks who want to look this up - but apparently in very old trolley cars, energy was stored in a spinning drum that connected to an electric motor. The inertial moment of that drum was apparently large enough to carry sufficient energy to move folks from place to place in a city. A curious bit of history that this experiment reminds me of. 
Perhaps a flywheel connected to those rubber bands might work to stabilize output? Always interesting to watch what you come up with, Robert - and more often than not - mindblowing! :D

author: @aquarionh2o132
text:
Hi Robert,

I’m a relatively new subscriber to your channel here, and I love so much of what you do, produce, and conceive.  I too am an “out of the box” thinker, engaging in work from exotic chemistry to isolating various isotopes, to welding, carpentry and my other fields.  Out of curiosity, what is your “day job”? (Alternatively, if your YouTube channel has become your full time job, what did you do before YouTube?)

Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am a research chemist mate

replies.author: @aquarionh2o132
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith That’s a find field, especially when thinking outside the box.  I recently put a young (45 yrs old) chemistry professor to shame by isolating an isotope in my carport that he stated either was “impossible” to isolate or would require $100,000 in specialized equipment to attempt it.  It cost me $200  to do it in my carport, and the sample was confirmed via mass spectrometry analysis.  He was baffled.🤣. He never studied the research done back in the 1930s (and they may well not be “on the books” anymore...not sure on that one).

author: @PetraKann
text:
In reality, if you do the sums, pumped Hydro is not cheap. In fact it’s typically a waste of 70% of the energy needed to operate it (even though it does store energy in the form of potential)
To be fair there is always an energy loss when energy is transferred or transported. (Leaving aside superconductors)

author: @AnalystPrime
text:
This reminds me of the guy who realized that if you are just making a simple light for some mud hut so people don't need to burn stuff to see they don't need expensive batteries and solar panels. Just connect a LED to a motor that has couple gears connected to a wheel with rope around it. You lift a weight, like a bag of sand, gravity pulls it down turning the motor and you get light for maybe twenty minutes.
Sure it would be great to have a flashlight you can carry in your hand or solar panels that can charge batteries and run bigger appliances, but a simple and extremely cheap ceiling light is good enough to start with and can be mass produced easier.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I couldn't agree more mate - a solution to a problem is a solution to a problem not a solution that you think is the problem and may very well not be

author: @Alexander_Sannikov
text:
when DC motor is rotated, it creates a pulsing DC, not AC. it's easy to verify: just connect it to a different DC motor and it will spin too, which's only possible for DC input. another way to prove it is just by connecting to an oscilloscope.

so if anything, you want to smooth that pulsing AC with a capacitor to make it smoother, but it does not need a rectifier.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure - thanks mate

author: @Alexander_Sannikov
text:
3:20 According to guiness's world records, the record flight time of rubberband airplane is 2m 7.37s. That's certainly not 2 and a half hours. 
Still, it's always worth considering energy density per unit volume, per unit mass, etc, for very different means of storing energy, including chemical, kinetic, elastic. There can be some surprises there.

author: @michaelbrear2214

As a young teenager I used to read a small ' comic' strip in one of the tabloid newspapers (I forget which paper) which featured new or interesting inventions and innovations. One of these was a material that would contract with great force if immersed in a saline solution and expand again if immersed in ordinary water. This was touted as a way of generating electricity in coastal areas but then like many things I never heard anything more! 
I would be interested if you or anyone else could enlighten me.
Kind regards

author: @Killianwsh
text: Great message Rob!  I'm a huge believer in the notion getting people to think of simple analog mechanical elements, arranged in the least wear/friction/number of moving parts, methodology will lead us (Mankind) to our "Holy Grail" (for this era) of simple, clean, readily available energy and energy storage that EVERYONE can EASILY make and use!  I think your 110% correct that we MUST find means to engage the youth to think about science & engineering from new angles that break the dogmatic paradigms that have been shoved at us through an often "profit protecting funnel and blinders"  teaching methodology!  Whats more, I truly believe that the secrets to a huge number of incredibly efficent machines have been preserved in the simple mechanical motion or "pull-string" type toys of history!  There really is an incredible treasure trove of M.E. genius tucked away in children's toys that's simple, inexpensive, and TOUGH!..(lol because...KIDS!  :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely mate and nicely put - cheers

author: @deslomeslager
text:
We always think of a survival situation. We will do our uttermost best to get a radio to work. There is no electricity. Hmmm. How much more then static would we hear if we manage to get a radio to work? Once the sun bursts out an EMP, and later on a second one, we will live without any electricity and/or devices. Hard to imagine (out of the box, waaaaay out of the box).
But your intro was clear. We do need to think outside of the box, a narrow vision does keep you in a sort of loop. PS: my way of going out of the box is just brainstorming with someone else, that works the best for me. And I know you do the same. That is what all the comments are for!
I once found a guy (I think on OU dot com) who took 2 peltier elements and stuck those on his arm (with an aluminum backplate). He was able to have an LED shining continuously on his bodyheat. Not efficient, but a nice try anyway.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh yeah mate - the comments and interacting with everyone is a great thing

replies.author: @tg_privat
replies.text: I was playing with old laptop cells. After a few years I ended up with a small 4S9P battery, charging it with solar each day. And now I only use it for charging every possible tool in house out of it. So all my remotes, phones and other gadgets run on solar power. :-) Also charging power drills and lawn mower. But it is AC charged, when the solar pannels work. 750W panels on 500W microinverter.

author: @G-ra-ha-m
text:
Great video, science and reality are not hugely encouraged these days but inevitably it will be needed. I vaguely recall some spinning flywheels being used to store electricity in some time ago.
I read recently that Australia had paid Elon Musk a great deal of money to fill up a bit of land with Lithium Ion batteries, which illustrated to me the lack of knowledge at all levels. Lithium ion as you know is good for cars and model helicopters and drones, but rather short lived, fire prone and pointlessly lightweight for storing on the ground on a giant continent. 
The number of alternate chemistries available that would last far longer and could be made from ores already mined in Australia is huge - but apparently most politicians don't just miss the 'home economics' courses as a kid, but the basic 'engineering 101' of selecting _the right tool for the job_ , which they usually mistake as themselves...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think you are exactly right there mate - they are thinking down a track so their choices are narrow

author: @threeMetreJim
text:
I've come up with a way of using a common waste item. I was making a some other device (trying to make something very cheaply out of waste for a different purpose) and found you can make some decent blades for a micro wind generator from a common 2 litre carbonated soft drink bottle. The material is pretty tough (PET) and the natural curve formed by the diameter of the bottle gives it some strength for use as a micro generator blade. It would probably be made stronger by heating the bottle to shrink it (and thicken it) a little. Might also be useful for making small vibrating blades (fluttering) for the vibrating magnet and coil generator.
You might even be able to make a small vertical turbine by cutting slots into a 2 litre bottle, and then pulling the bottom towards the top (string through the bottom and cap - or a threaded rod) to make something resembling the chimney topper anti-downdraught things, but I haven't tried that yet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a set of really nice ideas mate - I am going to give it a go - do you mind if I do a video if it works?

replies.author: @threeMetreJim
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Not at all, I had a look round, and it seems there are a lot of bottle 'spinners'. I tried the slots in bottle and couldn't use string in a single central hole as the PET material is too springy (it makes a good locking spring when it bounces back).It tends to go off to one side. Making micro turbine blades is ok, I'm using blades cut from a 2 litre bottle to break the fall of a device that needs to spin (a version of the old toy helicopter, sycamore seed thing, but with multiple real uses, hopefully). I put the blades in the window of a car I was travelling in and they spun quite well and didn't deform at 30MPH. Thickening the material by heating might make even stronger blades, if needed. The PET material can bend quite easily but seems to be very strong for shear forces (good for a rotating turbine blade mount).

replies.author: @threeMetreJim
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It''s taking multiple attempts to get things just right, but closing in slowly.

replies.author: @threeMetreJim
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Just shrunk the centre section of a PET bottle. Used two food tins as a former. Takes patience and a heat gun, but the result is smooth and radius reduced by about 10mm. Unfortunately I used corrugated tins so the PET now needs to be cut off. A smooth former would be better and there might be an easier way than carefully using a heat gun for around 10 mins to get a nice finish.

replies.author: @threeMetreJim
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If you want to go all industrial on PET, it seems feasable to use a bottle pre-form and blow mould it into the shape of a blade (a small one about 30cm length). You then get a screw on blade that is very lightweight (less of a hazard if the blade fails) but quite strong for a micro generator.

author: @gr1f1th
text: mechanical storage via flywheels and solar steam are the methods I keep drifting back to.  My favorite way of thinking of improving things is taking two systems and incorporating them together for efficiency. Like the new graphene solar water distiller making low energy steam then boosted in temp for pressure going to a tesla turbine.  The tesla turbine magnetically coupled to a flywheel in a vacuum to store the low torque energy of the turbine. Fun to think about.... but then I go and buy a 300 watt solar panel and batteries... and it's all solid state.

author: @blg53
text: Hi Rob,  someone suggesting a flywheel took the words out of my mouth. That would be quite an interesting modification. Instead of winding up "the other end" hook it to a stiff spring. Thread the rubber through the holes of a flywheel and wind up the flywheel. This would store the energy in the rubbers, but also in the spring as it will inevitable get stretched by winding up of the flywheel. When you let it go it should be able to unwind and oscillate several times before stopping.

author: @pulesjet
text:
Surgical Tubing would provide a much more consistent force.  Use of a Centrifugal governor
 would provide yet more consistency.  The Rubber Need not be Twisted to store your energy.  It can be simply Stretched.  Say Stretched around a large pulley. Sugical tubing Stretched around a large pully or drum as said pulley is wound around.  This method would provide a more constant pulling force.  regulated via the centrifugal governor .  Use that to drive your Alternator set up. Wish we could post a drawing here.     https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centrifugal_governor

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
If you look at the Jeorg Sprave slingshot channel, you can see a whole lot of high power rubber band energy use, although not the same, but if you used a thick wide rubber band strip wound up like a clock spring, you could in theory save up a whole lot of power compactly and [relatively] quietly, especially if you used very fine toothed [or even rubber] gear/roller(s) to compound your shaft speeds and extend the useful harvesting of the power over an efficient range of rotational generator speeds.

For an example, just imagine [if you have ever seen this, I have as a child, very fascinating] pulling the ticker out of a normal wind up clock...notice that the little wheel at the end spins quite fast for a decent amount of time, albeit without very much strength, still, depending on the power stored in your elastic, and the speed/power ratio [the mathematically adjusted horsepower-into-wattage conversion factor] you gear your elastic spring to, you could in theory store a very high amount of time, in a low amount of power, or a lower amount of time, and a higher amount of power, but if you wound it with something like a water wheel, or a wind mill, [or both, why wait on one if you can get both?] your amount of emergency power storage could indeed be phenomenal.

Please do keep in mind, of course, that elastic has a limited life span [although various chemical elastics have differing life spans, and differing elastic properties, and some do not hold up to petroleum or vegetable oil, so by all means be sure to investigate a myriad of elastics/elastomers] so you may wish to have some sealed spares on hand, or, in the case of a post apocalyptic world [as in a science fiction book (or at least we hope ONLY in such a setting!)] one should know where in nature to harvest elastomers, or else how to synthesize them from something they can readily get their hands on, which will also store well, especially if it is in limited quantities.

This is why I originally opted for compressed air. It never goes bad, it is never in short supply, and it takes no special skills to obtain other than a well built mechanism to compress it into a highly reusable container.

Imagine if you could do so much as to build an elastic driven, elastic thermal refrigerator so that the mechanism were driven by wind or water or both [and 'water' need not be something so isolinear as a creek or river! Rain water off of a roof into a tank, water into a pond, or the motion energy in waves, all qualify as water in motion! ~( 'w')/ ]

Winding up one's stored energy is not super difficult to obtain, depending on the side of the mechanism, one could have an animal such as a goat, sheep, cow or horse [sled dogs even?] walk around a long pole and drive their generator, using the elastic as a power buffer to ensure smoother, more constant power at the output shaft so as to reduce or eliminate power surges and brown-outs.

Happy experimenting people!

       /)/) 
     (   'ˬ') 
@(>_ )-b 
      \) \)

author: @captainscarlet6758
text: I thought the planet was a giant battery. Why cant we funnel the energy from a bolt of lightning into a capacitor and use that. Would we have to slow the rate that the capacitor absorbed the electricity, would it explode? We could then store it in a battery. At the moment it just goes to ground and is wasted. Anything that creates movement be it wind or gravity, you have electricity. So many smart people about and no-one has done this yet, Just my thoughts. I like your videos, thanks for posting.

replies.author: @Draakdarkmaster6
replies.text:
because its a ton of volts at almost no amps, which translates to basically no wattage since volts x amps = watts, its why alot of people survive lightning strikes, because the voltage is far more than enough to pass through skin, but the amperage isn't necessarily enough to kill in alot of cases (which only needs to be 0.1-0.2 amps) the amount of voltage in a static discharge if you touch someone and shock them by accident is between 20000 and 25000 volts but with almost no amps so it hurts, but never kills. 
obviously there's some amps in lightning strikes but unfortunately its nearly impossible to catch it without massive resistors or risking one or all of your components exploding or getting fried, but there is an alternative that is essentially the same thing by far more controlled called ion harvesting, where they collect ambient electricity from lower atmosphere with extremely tall towers using essentially graphene coated wire with micro hairs on the exterior, because electricity is drawn to sharper points over broader objects (its why trees get struck by lightning alot) so the charged particles gather on the graphene before the area is charged enough to discharge into a lightning bolt and the harvest rate is actually kind of amazing, even on a completely clear day, and there's a company trying to develop it in larger scale. so its *like* catching lightning, but before it becomes a bolt, and more constantly.

replies.text: or we could change our method - I like atmosphere motors because they run from potential difference and not amps

author: @electronresonator8882
text:
there's also Nitinol, store energy in form of shape memory,  
about post apocalyptic world, my idea is using modified hand pull flywheel toy, attach magnets on the flywheel and turn it into a small generator... or using geothermal activity and use Stirling engine to run a generator

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I love Stirling engines

author: @maxchill308
text: Rubber bands, duct tape, and WD-40 are essential elements. With these simple things any creative & curious person can solve all the mysteries of the universe.😵 That said, it's a nice day to go fishing for my supper. 🎣

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol 100% agree mate - nothing you can't do with enough duct tape lol

author: @The-Green-Manali-shi
text: Again, excellent. Who doesn't want to hear you talk? Who needs caffeine when you could keep us up all night with words of magic? You took me back to my childhood, too many years away, unfortunately. We used to make a tank out of an empty cotton reel, a slice of candle, a rubber band and two matchsticks. The candle acted as a slow release of power from the wound up rubber band.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I remember that mate! loved them I used to cut little notches in rim as tracks

replies.author: @The-Green-Manali-shi
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So did I.

author: @onemanriflemaker3873
text:
I hit subscribe within ten seconds when I first watched a video of yours.. 
your ethos of encouraged experimentation is spot on. 
You have to be interested to be interested. 
If I could find the money. @ 46 years old I would study chemistry at degree level. 
Such is your inspiration.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you - my friend - are a star

replies.author: @55Ramius
replies.text: I also subbed on first video I saw. The vibrating generator made from a speaker. Love the use of common things to do projects. I am 65 and never lost my interest in science and engineering. I did lose my keys though.....

replies.author: @onemanriflemaker3873
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith one day, if your still willing. I plan to come up for that chat we had planned three years ago, before I got struck down with pneumonia.

author: @idea-shack
text: Have you seen the youtube video where the guy builds a refrigerator based on a cycle of contracting and expanding rubber bands? The band actually warms up a little and cools between a stretch and relaxation and this was the basis of heat pump for him. You should check it out.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Fun stuff.  You could consider a flywheel like the Formula 1 KERS. There have been school engineering competitions using metal tension and/or torsion springs and bungee cord.  Not too many years ago there were wind-up torches ("flashlights" U.S.) and shavers on the market -- some from Switzerland where clockwork is historically developed to quite excellent engineering.

author: @ionode
text: When i Read battery from elastic bands i instantly thought: triboelectric effect, van de Graaff generator. And as you stated i was thinking inside my box, never thought you'd make mechanical batterys after all the successes you had booked in the chemical/physical  batteries. Thanks for sharing all your Passion and knowledge on these subjects. Ive enjoyed your channel for a long time now, Kind regards from a dutch 'youngling'

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - and bless your heart

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: What about a Tesla turbine or something using compressed air as a storage system?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like compressed air engines mate

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yeah throwing out ideas for ya :) I have thought of this for many years trying to think of an efficient rotary drive that is not passive in any way. Then use a compressor used for scuba gear for compressed air storage

replies.author: @Web3Dre
replies.text: Could construct a tall shaft with a heavy airtight block with a one way valve. Use a pulley system to lift the block and let it slowly fall down the shaft making high air pressure. Then connect a tesla turbine to the bottom of the shaft with a generator. Should be able to store and generate allot of power on demand that way.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: @Web3Dre Nice!!
